story:
He should have known something was wrong when he looked back and saw the snow behind him unmarked, but fear and exhaustion blurred the truth. He followed the cheerful piping of an unseen pipe that slipped through his skin and warmed him enough to run through winter. Snow sifted from a torn cotton sky and hissed on the flames eating his village.

Run, his mother had rasped, shoving him out the back door toward the pens. He ran, slipped in snow and muck, then turned, ashamed to have left her though she no longer looked like his mother. Once the village belle, she had bloomed with red lesions, an army of ants under her skin. Her hair came away in clumps; fire and knives could not wring a cry from her. Yet her eyes stayed calm and blue, a cool cloth on fever, and her love held him even when she dared not touch him. When lesions rose along his own back like a second spine, she alone wept and stroked his face.

When he crept home through the shadows, he found only a dark smear dragged from the kitchen to the bedroom. In the bedroom doorway stood a man wrapped in layers of cloth and charms against infection, a sword dripping red in his fist. The boy hid until the man turned, then fled into the streets where horses pounded and soldiers flung torches.

He fell once when something sharp struck his head.

He woke to snow in his nose and smoke in his lungs. Fires crackled under a chorus of screams. The snow around him was wine-dark. He staggered for the trees.

Now he ran, amazed his matchstick legs outran armored horses and arcs of thrown fire. He wished for his father’s ax. He wished he had not left, had not seen that smear on the floor. He wished someone had come to heal, not to erase.

The forest still carried echoes—women dragged through burning doorways, men cut down, children felled by thrown fire—but above them the pipe crooned, promising all a hurt boy could need, and toward it he ran. With every step he felt stronger. When the screams thinned, he understood the wordless song: snows melting; sun like a knit sweater; a father waking with color and laughter; a mother’s skin smooth and warm, smelling of vanilla and bread; brothers and sisters teasing, dancing, telling stories, lifting him when he fell; a world where bad things could not enter, where a boy might grow old, weathered, and well-loved.

He ran faster, almost flying, greedy for that world. Through the trees he glimpsed the piper, who danced as he played, now ahead, now behind, now at his side. A black hood hid his face. Beneath it flashed pied clothes in bright patches whenever the wind snapped back the cloak. His pipe—and the fingers that moved upon it—were bone white. And just like the boy, he left no footprints in the snow.
